name: Dodona Zeus
description: Dodona was one of the oldest oracular sites in Greece, dedicated to Zeus (also known as Zeus Naios or Zeus Bouleus) and Dione. Situated in Epirus, in northwestern Greece, it was believed to be a place where mortals could communicate with the divine through the rustling of oak leaves, the cooing of doves, and the murmuring of a sacred spring. The priests and priestesses interpreted these natural phenomena to deliver prophecies from Zeus. The oracle at Dodona predates the oracle at Delphi and held immense significance throughout ancient Greece. Pilgrims traveled from far and wide to seek guidance on matters of importance, ranging from personal dilemmas to affairs of state. The sacred oak tree, at the heart of the sanctuary, was considered the dwelling place of Zeus himself, and its rustling leaves were the primary means of divine communication. The site was eventually superseded in importance by Delphi but remained a respected place of worship and consultation for centuries, playing a crucial role in shaping the religious and political landscape of ancient Greece.
subtitle: Ancient Oracle of Zeus in Epirus

keyMyths.title: The Founding of the Oracle
keyMyths.description: According to legend, the oracle was founded by doves that flew from Egypt. One dove landed in Libya and established the oracle of Ammon, while the other landed in Dodona and proclaimed that an oracle of Zeus should be established there.

keyMyths.title: Zeus's Sacred Oak
keyMyths.description: The rustling of the leaves of the sacred oak tree was believed to carry the voice of Zeus. Priests and priestesses interpreted these sounds to answer questions and provide guidance to those seeking counsel from the god.

sources.author: Herodotus
sources.description: A detailed account of the history and customs of various cultures, including the story of the oracle's founding.
sources.text: A detailed account of the history and customs of various cultures, including the story of the oracle's founding.
